coredata.dc:title: Green-emitting carbon quantum dots as efficient fluorescent probes for Cu2+ and EDTA detection by “turn-on-off” strategy
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Qian, Xing
Wang, Zijing
Chen, Zeyu
El-Bahy, Salah M.
Li, Dongxiang
Qin, Lianjie
Li, Chunfang
El-Bahy, Zeinhom M.
Zhao, Jikuan
coredata.dc:description: Copper is essential for human body because it plays an important role in various physiological processes, so the sensitive detection of Cu2+ ions is a vital topic in human health and environment safe. In this work, green-emitting carbon quantum dots (CQDs) facilely synthesized by a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method were used as fluorescent probes for Cu2+ detection. The CQDs showed a rapid and sensitive response to Cu2+ with a color change and brightened fluorescence. The fluorescence enhancement of CQDs reaches 4.51-fold by 5 μM Cu2+ as a typical “turn-on” strategy. A good linear relation of the fluorescence enhancement by Cu2+ appears in a range of 0.1–1 μM Cu2+ with a limit of detection (LOD) of 2.46 nM. Then, the enhanced fluorescence of Cu2+-CQDs could be rapidly decreased by EDTA via a quenching response (“turn-off”). A linear range of the fluorescence decrease and EDTA concentration is 1–10 μM with a LOD of 0.19 μM. The interference experiments for the detections of both Cu2+ and EDTA show a high selectivity. The practical detections of both Cu2+ and EDTA in actual water samples and spiked samples demonstrated good reproducibility and effective recovery rates. In addition, the Cu2+-CQDs even had a slow “turn-off” response to histidine over 20 natural amino acids. Overall, the CQDs-based “turn-on-off” detection methods with high sensitivity and selectivity in this work can be considered a promising new choice for the fluorescence analysis of Cu2+ and EDTA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescent probes
Cu(II)
EDTA
Fluorescence enhancement
Quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Cu2+ by CQDs as fluorescent probes
Detection of EDTA by Cu2+-CQDs as fluorescent probes
Practical application of actual water samples and spiked samples
Detection for Cu2+ by CQDs as fluorescence probes
Detection for EDTA by Cu2+-CQDs as fluorescence probes
Detection practices of actual water samples and spiked samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NNSFC 21975139), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2017MB042), China. Also the authors extend their appreciation to Taif University, Saudi Arabia for supporting this work through project number (TU-DSPP-2024-20).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical photos of dilute CQDs without and with 15 μM Cu2+ under daylight or a 365 nm UV lamp (A), the 3-D fluorescence spectra of CQDs without (B) and with 15 μM Cu2+ (C), the fluorescence lifetime (D) and the measured data for quantum yield calculation (E) of dilute CQDs without and with 15 μM Cu2+, and the UV–vis spectra of CQDs with 0–25 μM Cu2+ (F). The inset in (F) shows the change of absorbance at 400 nm.
Photo of CQDs with 5 μM different cations (A), the fluorescence response of CQDs to different cations in the absence and presence of Cu2+ (B) and that to Cu2+ in different copper salts (C).
Fluorescence intensity change of CQDs with Cu2+ concentration in ranges of 0.5–25 μM (A) and 0.1–1.0 μM (B), respectively.

The fluorescence response of Cu2+-CQDs to 20 μM common anions, multi-dentate ligands, and physiological molecules in the absence and presence of 20 μM EDTA (A), the fluorescence intensity change of Cu2+-CQDs in the presence of 2–30 μM EDTA (B) and 1–10 μM EDTA (C), respectively.
The fluorescence lifetime τ 1, τ 2, and average τ of CQDs and Cu2+-CQDs.
Comparison of sensitive CQDs-based fluorescent probes for Cu2+ detection*.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: PET: photo-induced electron transfer, SQE: static quenching effect, DQE: dynamic quenching effect.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of Cu2+ in actual water samples and spiked samples by the CQDs.
Detection of EDTA in actual water samples and spiked samples by Cu2+-CQDs.

head.ce:title: Green-emitting carbon quantum dots as efficient fluorescent probes for Cu2+ and EDTA detection by “turn-on-off” strategy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Lab of Colloids and Functional Nanostructures, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology (QUST)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Copper is essential for human body because it plays an important role in various physiological processes, so the sensitive detection of Cu2+ ions is a vital topic in human health and environment safe. In this work, green-emitting carbon quantum dots (CQDs) facilely synthesized by a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method were used as fluorescent probes for Cu2+ detection. The CQDs showed a rapid and sensitive response to Cu2+ with a color change and brightened fluorescence. The fluorescence enhancement of CQDs reaches 4.51-fold by 5 μM Cu2+ as a typical “turn-on” strategy. A good linear relation of the fluorescence enhancement by Cu2+ appears in a range of 0.1–1 μM Cu2+ with a limit of detection (LOD) of 2.46 nM. Then, the enhanced fluorescence of Cu2+-CQDs could be rapidly decreased by EDTA via a quenching response (“turn-off”). A linear range of the fluorescence decrease and EDTA concentration is 1–10 μM with a LOD of 0.19 μM. The interference experiments for the detections of both Cu2+ and EDTA show a high selectivity. The practical detections of both Cu2+ and EDTA in actual water samples and spiked samples demonstrated good reproducibility and effective recovery rates. In addition, the Cu2+-CQDs even had a slow “turn-off” response to histidine over 20 natural amino acids. Overall, the CQDs-based “turn-on-off” detection methods with high sensitivity and selectivity in this work can be considered a promising new choice for the fluorescence analysis of Cu2+ and EDTA.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Green-emitting carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can be used as fluorescence probes for Cu2+ detection through fluorescence enhancement (“turn-on”) strategy, meanwhile the enhanced fluorescence of Cu2+-CQDs can be quenched by EDTA (“turn-off”) as an EDTA detection method. The linear range in Cu2+ detection is 0.1–1 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 2.46 nM, while it is 1–10 μM with a LOD of 0.19 μM in EDTA detection. The practical application in actual water samples and spiked samples demonstrates good reproducibility and effective recovery rates, showing a high accuracy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper is an essential trace element in human body and plants, it plays an important role in various physiological processes [1–3]. However, a long-term exposure to copper will cause liver or kidney damage, and the deficiency or excess of copper can lead to a series of serious diseases [4,5]. Nowadays, copper is widely used in fields of battery conductors, metal coatings, metallurgy, and chemical industry [6–8], the discharge of industrial wastewater makes rivers and soil susceptible to be polluted by Cu2+ as a heavy metal [9,10]. This not only causes serious environmental damage, but also harms plant growth, and even poses a great danger to human health with a significant disease risk. Therefore, it is vital to improve the selectivity and sensitivity of Cu2+ determination for environmental and medical applications [11–13].

In the past years, ion detection by fluorescence analysis has been widely developed due to its advantages of fast response, simple operation and high sensitivity [14–20], various fluorescent probes have been continuously developed to improve the detection sensitivity and selectivity [21–26]. Particularly, because carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have the advantages of stable luminescence, good biocompatibility, easy synthesis, low cost and low toxicity [27–29], they are regarded as promising fluorescent probes for selective detection of metal ions [30–32], biomolecules [33], inorganic salts [34], and organic amines [35,36]. In the major published works, the “turn-off” and “turn-on” strategies of fluorescence signals were frequently used in the detection practice, respectively corresponding to the fluorescence quenching and fluorescence enhancing or recovering [36–40].

Recently, the CQDs-based fluorescent probes for Cu2+ detection experienced a rapid development [41–49], in which the detection mechanism was mainly attributed to the “turn-off” strategy (quenching). The quenched fluorescence reveals a strong interaction between Cu2+ and CQDs, so Cu2+ ions should be closely attached to the surface of CQDs, and the Cu2+-attached CQDs are usually denoted as Cu2+-CQDs. Subsequently, the Cu2+-CQDs were developed to construct new detection methods through the fluorescence recovery response (“turn-on”) to special ligand analytes [46–54]. In our opinion, the selectivity of Cu2+-CQDs to ligand analytes should depend on the complicated interactions between CQDs, Cu2+, and ligand analytes, but the CQDs prepared by different methods have their own unique properties which are determined by the synthesis conditions of precursors, solvents, temperature, and protective agent, as well as the post-modification. Therefore, the Cu2+-CQDs as fluorescence probes have different selectivity to the ligand analytes from glutathione [49–51], oxalate ions [46,47], L-cysteine [48], cyanide [52], pyrophosphate (PPi) [53] to sinapine [54] though all of these analytes similarly result in the fluorescence recovery of quenched Cu2+-CQDs.

However, only few work utilized the fluorescence enhancement (“turn-on”) strategy for Cu2+ detection till now [55], and no one used a “turn-off” strategy for the subsequent detection of ligand analytes by Cu2+-CQDs. In this work, green-emitting CQDs were employed as fluorescence probes for Cu2+ detection through fluorescence enhancement (“turn-on”). Then, the enhanced fluorescence of Cu2+-CQDs was quenched by EDTA (“turn-off”) as a detection method of EDTA. Additionally, the Cu2+-CQDs were also found to have a slow “turn-off” response to histidine selectively. Such green-emitting CQDs are expected as efficient fluorescence probes in the detection of Cu2+ and EDTA.

L-cystine and uric acid were purchased from Macklin Reagent, China. Sodium citrate, sodium pyrophosphate, sodium L-ascorbate, 3,4-diaminobenzoic acid (DABA), DL-lipoic acid, glutathione (GSH), and dithiothreitol (DTT) were obtained from Aladdin Reagent, China. The kit of 20 natural L-amino acid standards was purchased from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd., China. Common inorganic salts, NaOH, sodium tartrate, sodium succinate, sodium acetate, disodium edetate (EDTA) and disodium oxalate in analytical grade were purchased commercially. All chemicals were used directly without further purification, and ultrapure water (≥18.2 MΩ) was used in the experiments.

The CQDs were facilely synthesized by a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method according to our previous works [56,57]. In detail, L-cystine (1.0 g) was dissolved in 10 mL NaOH solution (0.95 mol/L) under ultrasonication, then the carbonization reaction was conducted for 5 s at 135 °C on a single-mode microwave reactor (NOVA-2S, PreeKem Scientific, China). The pale yellow sample of CQDs was obtained after cooling to room temperature, which was diluted by 10 times (denoted as diluted CQDs) for the next fluorescence analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ by CQDs as fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: The common inorganic cations in 10.0 mM stock solutions were diluted to 100, 50 or 20 μM for next use in this work. First, 0.30 mL diluted CQDs, 1.1 mL H2O and 0.60 mL Cu2+ (CuSO4) solution (50 μM) were mixed to record 3-D fluorescence spectra. Second, 0.10 mL diluted CQDs, 1.4 mL H2O and 0.50 mL cation solution (20 μM) were mixed to record the 2-D fluorescence emission spectra excited at 400 nm for ion selectivity detection. Third, the interference test samples were prepared by mixing 0.10 mL diluted CQDs, 0.90 mL H2O, 0.50 mL one of the various cation solutions (20 μM) and 0.50 mL Cu2+ (20 μM). Moreover, Cu(NO3)2, CuBr2, and CuCl2 were used instead of CuSO4 to explore the influence of counterions. To quantitatively determine the response to Cu2+, 0.10 mL diluted CQDs was mixed with the calculated volumes of CuSO4 solution and water to 2.0 mL, in which the Cu2+ concentration was 0.5–25 μM or 0.1–1.0 μM. All the samples were characterized by 2-D fluorescence spectra excited at 400 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of EDTA by Cu2+-CQDs as fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: 9.0 mL diluted CQDs was mixed with 42 mL H2O, and 9.0 mL Cu2+ solution (100 μM) to obtain a pale yellow solution of Cu2+-CQDs (containing 15 μM Cu2+) with enhanced fluorescence. Then, 1.0 mL above solution was respectively mixed with 1.0 mL single analyte solution (40 μM) of 24 anions, glucose, uric acid, urea, DL-lipoic acid, GSH, DTT, and 20 natural amino acids to record the fluorescence emission spectra. It was found that the slight yellow color of Cu2+-CQDs disappeared rapidly after adding EDTA; meanwhile the fluorescence test demonstrated a quenching by EDTA. To quantitatively determine the quenching effect by EDTA, 1.0 mL above solution of Cu2+-CQDs was added with designed volumes of EDTA solution and H2O, in which the EDTA concentration in the final samples ranged in 2–30 μM or 1–10 μM. All the mixtures were characterized by the 2-D fluorescence spectra under the excitation at 400 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application of actual water samples and spiked samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the reliability of CQDs as fluorescent probes for Cu2+ detection, actual water samples of tap-water, snowmelt, and river water (locally collected in Qingdao, China) were used to displace ultrapure water in the sample preparation for fluorescence test, the content of actual water in these samples was 90% by volume. The spiked concentrations of Cu2+ are set to 0, 1, 2 and 5 μM to perform recovery rate tests. Similarly, the Cu2+-CQDs were used to detect EDTA in the above actual water samples and spiked samples, and the spiked EDTA concentrations are 0, 1, 2 and 5 μM to determine recovery rates by the fluorescence analysis.

The 3-D/2-D fluorescence spectra were collected on an F-2700 spectrometer (HITACHI, Japan) and the fluorescence lifetime measurement was performed on an FLS1000 transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK), the UV–vis spectra were collected on a TU-1901 spectrometer (Beijing Purkinje General, China). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were performed at 25 °C on MALVERN Zetasizer Nano-ZS 90.

The CQDs synthesized by the microwave-assisted method from L-cystine show a pale yellow color and emit strong green fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp (Supplementary Materials, Fig. S1A). The 3-D fluorescence spectrum of the as-obtained CQDs shows a maximum excitation at 422 nm (Fig. S1B), and the maximum emission at 505 nm is independent on the excitation [56,57]. However, the as-obtained CQDs did not show a visible response to dilute analytes because the trace amount of analytes nearly could not alter the fluorescence emission of high-concentration CQDs, so the diluted CQDs were utilized in the next fluorescence analysis.

As shown in Fig. 1A, the further diluted CQDs by 66.7 times is colorless with weak fluorescence emitting, the 3-D fluorescence spectrum of diluted CQDs in Fig. 1B displays a nearly conical mountain peak, which is close to an excitation-independent emission. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths are respectively 395 nm and 495 nm, which are different from those values (422 nm and 505 nm) of the as-obtained CQDs. This can be ascribed to the pH change after dilution [56]. However, when Cu2+ ions were added into the dilute CQDs, the 3-D fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 1C) shows an excitation-independent emission with largely enhanced intensity by 3.25-fold, meanwhile the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths show 5 nm redshift to 400 nm and 500 nm, respectively. The fluorescence enhancement proves a strong interaction between the CQDs and Cu2+, so that the Cu2+-attached CQDs can also be denoted as Cu2+-CQDs. According to the component analysis of the CQDs [56], ether, hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxylate, and little thiolate groups are grafted on carbon cores and unreacted L-cystine moieties are physically adsorbed on the surface of CQDs, so the carboxylate and thiolate on carbon cores should provide strong electrostatic attraction to Cu2+, as well as the carboxylate group in amino acid groups. This supposition has been proved by zeta potential measurement because the electrostatic adsorption of Cu2+ reverses the zeta potential from −42 mV of the CQDs to +49 mV of the Cu2+-CQDs, so the Cu2+ adsorption changes the surface charge state of the carbon dots.

The reported mechanisms for the fluorescence enhancement of CQDs involve intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) [58,59], fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), aggregation-induced emission (AIE) [60], and chelate fluorescence enhancement (CHEP) [61,62]. Herein, the FRET mechanism is excluded because only one fluorophore exists in the system. Although Cu2+ may be chelated by carboxylate groups on the carbon cores and amino acid groups of cystine on the surface of CQDs, the CHEP mechanism shall be not supposed because the slight yellow color of Cu2+-CQDs corresponds to an absorption at the excitation wavelength, which is different from those systems based on the CHEP mechanism [61,62].

To explore the mechanism of fluorescence enhancement, the measurements of particle size, fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield were performed in our work. The DLS results show a similar particle size of CQDs and Cu2+-CQDs, which excludes the AIE mechanism. As shown in Fig. 1D, the fluorescence lifetime curve of Cu2+-CQDs is slightly different from that of CQDs. The data τ 1, τ 2, and average τ listed in Table 1 show that the average lifetime of CQDs increases from 4.72 ns to 5.07 ns after attaching Cu2+, which should be ascribed to a non-radiative transition [58,59]. From Fig. 1E, the quantum yields of CQDs and Cu2+-CQDs are calculated to be 27.5% and 29.6%, respectively. According to the analysis of the 3-D fluorescence spectra, the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of Cu2+-CQDs show 5 nm redshift as compared with CQDs. All these results are in accordance with the ICT mechanism [58,59]. In addition, the UV–vis spectra of CQDs (Fig. 1F) show an emerging peak at 375 nm after adding Cu2+. The inset in Fig. 1F shows the absorbance change at the excitation wavelength (400 nm) with Cu2+ concentration, indicating a significantly improved absorption. In other similar works of the ICT mechanism [58,59], the sample absorption at excitation wavelength was also improved after adding analyte metal ions. Such enhanced absorption of Cu2+-CQDs at the excitation wavelength can directly strengthen the fluorescence emission even under the same quantum yield, let alone the higher quantum yield of Cu2+-CQDs herein. In the ICT mechanism, when Cu2+ is added into CQDs, it will be adsorbed on carboxylate and/or little thiolate groups on the carbon cores under weak baisc condition by electrostatic interation [56], so the zeta potentail of CQDs changes from negative to positive, being companied with the charge transfer between Cu2+ and CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection for Cu2+ by CQDs as fluorescence probes
ce:section.ce:para: The dilute CQDs were respectively mixed with one of 20 cations to investigate ion selectivity, where the CQDs were diluted by 200 times and the cation concentration was 5 μM in the final mixture for fluorescence tests. As shown in Fig. 2A, only Cu2+ (CuSO4) brings a slight yellow color. Correspondingly, Fig. 2B shows the F/F0 values of the mixtures of CQDs and different cations, where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities of CQDs without and with metal ions, respectively. It is found that only Cu2+ greatly enhances the fluorescence intensity by 4.51-fold (F/F0= 4.51), while for other metal ions the F/F0 value just varies between 0.91 (Li+) and 1.09 (Fe2+). This result demonstrates a significant selectivity of CQDs for Cu2+. Beacuse the surface of CQDs contains carboxylate, thiolate and amino acid groups [56], this high selectivity should be ascribed to the specific electrostatic interaction of Cu2+ with carboxylate and/or thiolate, as well as the coordination interaction between Cu2+ and the amino groups [63]. Next, when the CQDs were first mixed with another metal ion as interference and then Cu2+ (both interference ion and Cu2+ are 5 μM, finally), the F/F0 value alters from 4.28 (Pb2+) to 4.67 (Li+), basically consistent to the value for Cu2+ alone (deviation: ±3.1%). So, it is concluded that the CQDs as fluorescence probes for Cu2+ detection have significant selectivity and anti-interference ability. Furthermore, Cu(NO3)2, CuCl2, and CuBr2 were used instead of CuSO4 to explore the influence of counterions, the corresponding F/F0 values are respectively 4.50, 4.47, and 4.50 (Fig. 2C), the average F/F0 value for 4 copper salts is 4.49 with a deviation of 0.11%, so the influence of counterions in copper salts is negligible.

For a quantitative detection of Cu2+, the fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs containing 0.5–25 μM Cu2+ were recorded first. Fig. 3A shows the change of fluorescence intensity with Cu2+ concentration (Fig. S2A). It is found that the fluorescence is gradually enhanced in the range of 0.5–5 μM Cu2+, while the curve slope decreases in the range of 5–25 μM Cu2+, which can be ascribed to the saturation of Cu2+ to CQDs. Then, a narrow range of 0.1–1.0 μM Cu2+ was used to determine the limit of detection (LOD). As shown in Fig. S2B and Fig. 3B, it is found that the fluorescence intensity linearly increases with Cu2+ concentration, and the fitted line gives a linear equation of y=50.20+97.49x with R2 of 0.998 (R: the linear correlation coefficient). The LOD for Cu2+ detection by this method is calculated to 2.46 nM based on the formula LOD=3σ/m, where σ is the standard deviation of a blank measurement and m is the slope of the linear equation [64]. The results show that the CQDs have a sensitive response to Cu2+, so they can be considered sensitive fluorescent probes for Cu2+ detection. Herein, the related information of similar sensitive CQDs-based fluorescent probes for Cu2+ detection was listed in Table 2. It is found that the CQDs-based detection through the fluorescence enhancement in our work has a comparable LOD value to that of the most sensitive CQDs-based sensors reported previously [44,55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection for EDTA by Cu2+-CQDs as fluorescence probes
ce:section.ce:para: In previous works, fluorescence quenching was always observed in the attaching of Cu2+ to CQDs, and the fluorescence recovery of Cu2+-CQDs was subsequently used to detect special ligand analytes through a “turn-on” response [46–54]. In our work the detection of Cu2+ was based on the fluorescence enhancement of CQDs, in a view of reverse thinking the Cu2+-CQDs should have a “turn-off” response to potential ligand analytes. Therefore, the Cu2+-CQDs were mixed with various anions, multi-dentate ligands, and physiological molecules to find out the selectivity. We found that the Cu2+-CQDs rapidly lost the slight yellow color after adding EDTA, while they didn’t show visible change for other analytes. If F0 and F respectively represent the fluorescence intensities of Cu2+-CQDs before and after adding analytes, as shown in Fig. 4A, the F/F0 values should reveal the response of Cu2+-CQDs to the analytes. It is found that the F/F0 value of Cu2+-CQDs after adding EDTA significantly decreases to 0.424, indicating a significant quenching (“turn-off”); while it changes to 0.864 after adding PPi, showing a weak quenching response. However, for other analytes, the F/F0 value just ranges between 0.942 (Cl–) and 1.058 (uric acid) with a deviation of ±5.8%. Next, the interference experiments were performed by adding another analyte first and then EDTA, where both the analytes and EDTA are 20 μM and Cu2+ is 7.5 μM finally. The F/F0 values of Cu2+-CQDs after adding EDTA in the presence of interference analytes range from 0.419 (Cr2O7 2–) to 0.458 (ClO4 –), which are near to the value for EDTA only. Although the Cu2+-CQDs show a weak response to PPi, the total response to PPi and EDTA together has no difference to EDTA alone, thus PPi can be avoided as an interference. Therefore, the Cu2+-CQDs have a superb selectivity for EDTA detection. In the mechanism, EDTA has strong chelation ability and can readily capture Cu2+ from Cu2+-CQDs and form a more stable Cu2+-EDTA chelate due to the higher coordination stability [42], therefore a detection method for EDTA can be constructed based on the fluorescence quenching (“turn-off”) of Cu2+-CQDs.

The fluorescence spectra (Fig. S3A) of Cu2+-CQDs in the presence of 2–30 μM EDTA were recorded to collect the intensity change (Fig. 4B) for a quantitative analysis. The fluorescence intensity decreases rapidly in the range of 2–10 μM EDTA, which reveals a sensitive response of Cu2+-CQDs to EDTA. But in the range of 10–30 μM EDTA, the insensitivity of fluorescence quenching by EDTA should be attributed to the saturation of EDTA to Cu2+. Then, the fluorescence spectra (Fig. S3B) were measured in a narrow range of 1–10 μM EDTA to find the LOD. As shown in Fig. 4C, a good linear relation is observed in the EDTA detection with a LOD of 0.19 μM. This result is much better than those based on the fluorescence recovery for EDTA detection [67,68].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection practices of actual water samples and spiked samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further illustrate the potential use of CQDs for Cu2+ detection, the fluorescence analysis was conducted on three actual samples of tap water, meltsnow, and river water. In the preparation of fluorescence test samples, 90% of ultrapure water was replaced by the actual water. From the results in Table 3, Cu2+ is found to be absent in tap water, but it is 0.07 μM and 0.12 μM in meltsnow and river water, respectively. The presence of Cu2+ in snowmelt can be attributed to air pollution, while that in river water can be ascribed to precipitation and ground pollution. From the detection data of the spiked samples in the standard recovery tests (Table 3), the CQDs-based detection for Cu2+ in our work has effective recovery rates from 95.5% to 103.7% with a relative standard deviation from 0.48% to 4.10%, which indicates high accuracy.

Similarly, the standard recovery tests were also conducted on the three actual water samples and spiked samples in the EDTA detection by Cu2+-CQDs. From the detection results in Table 4, EDTA is not found in all actual water samples, and the data of the spiked samples display effective recovery rates from 98.5% to 102.5% with a relative standard deviation from 0.26% to 2.85%, which also indicates high accuracy.

In addition, the quenched fluorescence of silver nanoclusters by Cu2+ was restored by adding histidine with good selectivity over other amino acids [69], and the copper-doped CQDs were also reported as fluorescence probes to histidine via quenching [70]. So, the Cu2+-CQDs (containing 15 μM Cu2+) were volume-equally mixed with one of 20 natural amino acids (40 μM) to explore the fluorescence response. First, from the fluorescence spectra after adding histidine for about 10 min (Fig. S4A), an obvious fluorescence decrease of Cu2+-CQDs was found as compared with other amino acids, showing a quenching response (“turn-off”). The F/F0 value of Cu2+-CQDs with histidine decreases to 0.851 (Fig. S4B), while it only shifts between 1.031 (valine) and 0.982 (proline) for other amino acids. However, after 24 h the slight yellow color of Cu2+-CQDs with histidine was found to nearly disappear, so the fluorescence spectra of all these samples were tested again. The fluorescence intensity of the sample with histidine largely decreases (Fig. S4C), the F/F0 value of Cu2+-CQDs with histidine drops to 0.532 (Fig. S4D), but it only varies from 0.934 (leucine) to 1.024 (tyrosine) for other amino acids. Therefore, the fluorescence of Cu2+-CQDs can be quenched after a long-time interaction with histidine, showing a slow selective “turn-off” response.

In summary, facilely synthesized green-emitting CQDs were used as fluorescence probes for Cu2+ detection through the fluorescence enhancement (“turn-on”), then the Cu2+-CQDs were conducted to detect EDTA through the quenching response (“turn-off”). The sensitive response of CQDs to Cu2+ displays a good linear relation in a range of 0.1–1.0 μM Cu2+ with a LOD of 2.46 nM, whereas a linear relation of EDTA detection ranges in 1–10 μM with a LOD of 0.19 μM. The interference experiments show high selectivity in the detections of both Cu2+ and EDTA, the practical application in the Cu2+ and EDTA detections of actual water samples and spiked samples demonstrates good reproducibility and effective recovery rates. In addition, the Cu2+-CQDs also show a slow “turn-off” response to histidine over 20 natural amino acids. Overall, the high selectivity and sensitivity of this “turn-on-off” fluorescence analysis method will endow the green-emitting CQDs with a promising potential for accurate detection of Cu2+ and EDTA.

Xing Qian: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zeyu Chen: Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zijing Wang: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jikuan Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration. Zeinhom M. El-Bahy: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Investigation. Dongxiang Li: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Salah M. El-Bahy: Validation, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Chunfang Li: Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Lianjie Qin: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NNSFC 21975139), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2017MB042), China. Also, the authors extend their appreciation to Taif University, Saudi Arabia for supporting this work through project number (TU-DSPP-2024-20).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.134089.

Luminescence

Inorg. Chem. Front.

Joseph, Efficient dual-mode colorimetric/fluorometric sensor for the detection of copper ions and vitamin C based on pH-sensitive amino-terminated nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: effect of reactive oxygen species and antioxidants, Anal Bioanal Chem, 411 (2019) 2619-2633.
